title: Twitter / X Feed — System Design
description: Design a home-timeline system for a microblogging service: post a short message and have it appear in the feeds of millions of followers within seconds. The central tension is fanout-on-write vs fanout-on-read and how to reconcile them for celebrity accounts.

nodes.label: Mobile / Web Client
nodes.description: User's app; posts tweets and reads the home timeline.

nodes.label: Load Balancer
nodes.description: Terminates TLS and spreads requests across API servers.

nodes.label: Timeline API
nodes.description: Request-time service for posting tweets and reading timelines.

nodes.label: Tweet Write Service
nodes.description: Validates and persists a new tweet, then triggers fanout.

nodes.label: Timeline Read Service
nodes.description: Assembles a user's home timeline on read.

nodes.label: Fanout Service
nodes.description: Pushes a new tweet into each follower's timeline cache.

nodes.label: Fanout Queue
nodes.description: Buffers fanout work so posting stays fast and bursts absorb.

nodes.label: Social Graph Service
nodes.description: Resolves follower/following edges for fanout and reads.

nodes.label: Graph Store
nodes.description: Authoritative follower/following adjacency, sharded by user.

nodes.label: Tweet Store
nodes.description: Authoritative tweet records, sharded by tweet/author id.

nodes.label: Timeline Cache
nodes.description: Per-user precomputed home timeline (list of tweet ids).

nodes.label: Tweet Cache
nodes.description: Hot tweet contents by id, hydrated into timelines on read.

nodes.label: Media CDN
nodes.description: Serves images/video attached to tweets near the user.

nodes.label: Media Object Store
nodes.description: Durable blob storage for uploaded photos and video.

nodes.label: Ranking Service
nodes.description: Scores and reorders timeline candidates by relevance.

nodes.label: Search Index
nodes.description: Inverted index for tweet text and hashtag lookup.

capacityDiagram: F[Fanout ~1M writes/s avg, 10-50M per celebrity post]

functional: Post a tweet (text + optional media), visible to followers.
Read a home timeline: recent tweets from accounts you follow, newest-first or ranked.
Follow and unfollow accounts; the timeline reflects the current follow set.
View a single user's profile timeline (their own tweets).
Search tweets by keyword and hashtag (secondary).
nonFunctional: Read-heavy: home-timeline reads dominate writes by ~50-100:1.
Timeline read latency p99 < 200 ms — reads must be precomputed, not assembled from scratch.
A posted tweet should reach followers within a few seconds (eventual consistency is acceptable).
Highly available for reads; a brief fanout delay is tolerable, a read outage is not.
Scale to hundreds of millions of users and accounts with tens of millions of followers.

capacity.label: Tweets per second
capacity.note: Spikes far higher on major events.

capacity.label: Avg followers
capacity.note: Median is far lower; distribution is heavy-tailed.

capacity.label: Max followers (celebrity)
capacity.note: A single post can mean 100M timeline writes — the fanout problem.

capacity.label: Fanout writes/sec (avg)
capacity.note: 5K tweets/s × ~200 avg followers, before excluding celebrities from push.

capacity.label: Fanout writes (single celebrity post)
capacity.note: A 100M-follower post can't be pushed in a few seconds — exactly why celebrities are pulled, not pushed.

capacity.label: Timeline cache size
capacity.note: 800 ids × 250M active users, before cache overhead — drives the timeline cache memory budget.

capacity.label: Read-side hydration
capacity.note: Per request: fetch ~50 timeline ids, batch-hydrate from the tweet cache (target >95% hit), rank a small candidate set.

api.description: Post a new tweet. Returns immediately after persisting; fanout is async. Carries a client-generated idempotency key so a client retry can't create a duplicate tweet.
Read the authenticated user's home timeline (cursor-paginated).

Read a single user's profile timeline (their own tweets, newest-first, cursor-paginated). Served by an author-id/time range over the tweet store, then hydrated and visibility-filtered through the same read path as the home timeline; protected accounts are only visible to approved followers.
Follow an account. Adds a graph edge; future tweets fan out to this follower, and the target's recent tweet ids are lazily backfilled (or merged on read) so the new follow shows up immediately.
Unfollow an account. Removes the graph edge; the author's tweets are filtered out at hydrate time against the current follow graph, and an async job lazily cleans their ids from the cached timeline.

messages.label: 200 not following (ids filtered at read; async cleanup)

api.description: Search tweets by keyword or hashtag. Queries the inverted index (eventually consistent — newly posted tweets become searchable within seconds), hydrates matches through the tweet cache, and applies the same viewer-specific visibility filtering as the timeline (protected/suspended authors, blocks/mutes, tombstones).

dataModel.name: tweets
dataModel.note: Authoritative tweet records, sharded by author or tweet id.

dataModel.name: follows
dataModel.note: Directed social graph. Two indexes: by follower and by followee.

dataModel.name: home_timeline
dataModel.note: Derived per-user list of (tweet id, author id) pairs (in Timeline Cache, e.g. a capped Redis list). Carrying author_id lets the read path filter unfollows, mutes, and blocks without an extra store hit.

dataModel.name: users
dataModel.note: Profile + counters; is_celebrity flag drives the hybrid read path.

fields.name: is_protected
fields.type: bool (tweets visible to approved followers only)

dataModel.name: blocks_mutes
dataModel.note: Per-viewer relationship overrides applied at read time to filter authors out of a hydrated page.

technologyChoices.concern: Edge routing and stateless API services
tradeoff: Self-hosted edge proxies give precise control over routing, connection handling, and deploy shape, but the team owns scaling and failover.
Managed gateways and load balancers remove most edge operations, but provider limits and per-request cost matter at timeline-read scale.
Container platforms fit the stateless API, write, read, graph, and fanout services because capacity can be shifted independently by path.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed edge with autoscaling service targets removes most hand-built load-balancer health checking and surge-capacity plumbing.

technologyChoices.concern: Tweet store and author/profile timeline reads
tradeoff: Wide-column stores fit append-heavy tweets and author/time range reads, but secondary access patterns must be modeled explicitly.
Relational stores are excellent for early product velocity and constraints, but large author timelines and hot events usually force sharding or a wide-column move.
Managed KV or wide-column stores reduce shard operations, but cross-row transactions and ad hoc queries become deliberate design tradeoffs.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A store with native time-ordered partition keys and global replication reduces the need for a custom author-timeline index service.

tradeoff: Two adjacency indexes, follower-to-followee and followee-to-follower, make both read fan-in and write fanout efficient.
A graph database is attractive conceptually, but the hot path mostly needs sharded adjacency lists and predictable range scans.
Caching hot celebrity follower pages helps fanout, but the authoritative graph store still decides protected accounts, unfollows, blocks, and mutes.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed wide-column/KV store with conditional writes removes much of the custom graph-shard placement and failover code.

tradeoff: A durable log is best when fanout jobs need replay, ordered partitions, lag tracking, and multiple downstream consumers such as search indexing.
A queue is simpler for discrete fanout tasks, retries, visibility timeouts, and DLQs.
Managed messaging reduces broker operations, but high fanout bursts can expose per-partition throughput limits and per-message costs.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Native DLQs, visibility timeouts, retention, and lag metrics remove much of the custom retry and queue-age bookkeeping in step 2.

technologyChoices.concern: Timeline cache for derived home timelines
tradeoff: In-memory lists give the fastest timeline read and cheap capped-list writes, but cold-user rebuild and shard loss must be planned.
A durable KV or wide-column timeline store costs more per read but makes rebuilds and regional failover easier.
The cache should hold tweet ids and author ids, not full tweet bodies, so edits, deletes, mutes, and unfollows stay correct at hydrate time.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed Redis-compatible cache with clustering, backups, and failover removes much of the custom timeline-shard recovery work.

tradeoff: A shared tweet cache avoids copying full tweet bodies into every follower timeline and keeps hot celebrity tweets cheap to hydrate.
Redis/Valkey support richer structures and invalidation patterns, while Memcached is simpler for plain object hydration.
Cache misses must batch load from the tweet store and stay within the read-path p99 budget.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A high-hit-rate managed cache removes most pressure to denormalize tweet bodies into home timeline entries.

technologyChoices.concern: Celebrity policy, counters, and backfill state
tradeoff: A durable user/profile store should own the current celebrity flag and follower counters so read and write paths make the same fanout decision.
A stream of counter and classification changes lets workers adjust policy asynchronously without recomputing every follower timeline.
Redis counters are fast, but the canonical threshold decision should survive cache loss and regional failover.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A durable conditional-write store plus a counter-change stream removes the need for a hand-built lock service around celebrity-threshold transitions.

tradeoff: Object storage keeps media out of the tweet store and lets timeline reads return stable media references.
A CDN is mandatory for image and video fanout because timeline reads should not stream blobs through the API.
Self-hosted object storage can reduce unit cost at scale, but regional durability, purge, and edge-cache operations become team-owned.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed object storage plus CDN origin integration removes most custom blob-serving and cache-fill infrastructure from the read path.

tradeoff: A learned ranker improves feed quality, but model serving and feature fetches sit on the p99 read path.
Inline heuristic scoring is easier to operate, while a model-serving tier needs hard timeouts and a reverse-chronological fallback.
Feature stores or low-latency KV caches keep ranking fresh without forcing every request through heavy analytical storage.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed model endpoint with autoscaling and canary rollout removes much of the bespoke model-serving deployment machinery, but not the need for read-path fallbacks.

tradeoff: An inverted index makes hashtag and keyword search fast, but it is eventually consistent with tweet writes and tombstones.
A log-backed indexing pipeline gives replay and reindexing, while direct write-time indexing is simpler but harder to recover after index loss.
Search results still need tweet hydration and viewer-specific visibility filtering before rendering.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed search service reduces shard, replica, and query-cache operations, but it does not remove the need for tombstone and privacy filtering.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability for fanout lag, cache health, and read p99
tradeoff: Fanout queue age, worker error rate, DLQ growth, timeline-cache hit rate, tweet-cache hit rate, and ranker timeout rate need first-class dashboards.
Self-hosted Prometheus/Grafana gives portability, while managed observability reduces retention and alerting operations.
Tracing is especially useful because one timeline read crosses cache, tweet hydration, graph filtering, and optional ranking.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed metrics, tracing, and log retention remove much of the custom operational datastore needed to diagnose fanout delays and p99 regressions.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-write
patterns.what: Precompute each follower's timeline at post time by pushing the tweet id into their cached list.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy systems where reads must be cheap and writers have manageable fanout.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-read
patterns.what: Assemble the timeline at read time by pulling recent tweets from each followee and merging.
patterns.whenToUse: When a writer has enormous fanout (celebrities) so push would be wasteful.

patterns.name: Hybrid fanout
patterns.what: Push for normal accounts, pull for celebrities, merge at read time.
patterns.whenToUse: Real social graphs with a heavy-tailed follower distribution.
patterns.group: Hybrid Fanout: Push for the Many, Pull for the Few

patterns.name: Async work queue
patterns.what: Offload fanout to a queue so posting returns fast and bursts are absorbed.
patterns.whenToUse: Any expensive side-effect that need not block the user's request.

patterns.name: Cache-aside hydration
patterns.what: Store ids in the timeline, hydrate full tweet bodies from a separate hot cache on read.
patterns.whenToUse: When the same tweet appears in millions of timelines — store it once, reference by id.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Assemble Timeline on Every Read
description: Start with the simplest correct design so the bottleneck is obvious.
On a home-timeline read, look up who the user follows, query the tweet store for each followee's recent tweets, merge them newest-first, and return.
This is correct and trivially consistent — but every read does a fan-in across hundreds of followees against the tweet store.

view.caption: On each read the timeline read service pulls the user's followees from the social graph and fetches their recent tweets straight from the tweet store.

steps.decisionPrompt: If 300K reads/sec each fan-in across ~200 followees, what load does that put on the tweet store?
whyNow: You must show you understand the read-heavy nature before optimizing — otherwise the interviewer can't tell whether your caching is principled or cargo-culted.

recap.newRisk: Every read is a fan-in of hundreds of queries; the tweet store melts at scale.

traps.trap: Defending read-time assembly as 'good enough' at scale.
traps.why: 300K reads/sec × ~200 followee lookups is ~60M tweet-store queries/sec — impossible on the authoritative store.
traps.instead: Acknowledge it as the baseline and move to precomputation.

steps.title: 2. Fanout-on-Write: Precompute Timelines
description: Flip the cost from read time to write time. When a user posts, look up their followers and push the new tweet id into each follower's cached timeline list.
Reads become a single cache read of a ready-made list of tweet ids — cheap and fast (p99 < 200 ms is now achievable).
Do the fanout asynchronously via a queue so posting returns immediately and traffic bursts are absorbed by workers.

view.caption: On posting, the write service persists the tweet and enqueues a fanout job; the fanout service reads followers from the social graph and pushes the tweet id into each follower's timeline cache.

options.name: Store tweet ids in the timeline (default)
options.description: On post, only the tweet id is written into each follower's timeline; reads later hydrate ids into full tweets via a shared cache.
pros: Each timeline entry is a tiny id, so timelines are cheap to store and fan out to millions of followers.
A tweet lives once in the tweet store; edits/deletes are reflected at hydrate time, not copied everywhere.
Hydration via a shared tweet cache means a hot tweet is cached once and reused across all timelines.
cons: Reads need a second hop to hydrate ids into full tweet bodies.
A hydration miss falls back to the tweet store, adding tail latency.
The read path is more complex than returning ready-made tweets.

view.caption: The fanout service pushes only the tweet id into each follower's timeline cache, leaving full tweet hydration to read time.

options.name: Store materialized full tweet copies
options.description: On post, the entire tweet body is copied into every follower's precomputed timeline, so reads return ready-to-render tweets with no hydration.
pros: Reads return ready-to-render tweets with no hydration hop — simplest, fastest read path.
No dependency on the tweet cache/store being warm at read time.
Each timeline is fully self-contained.
cons: Massive storage amplification: a popular tweet is copied into millions of timelines.
Edits and deletes must rewrite every copy across every follower timeline.
A hot tweet is duplicated everywhere instead of cached once.

view.caption: The fanout service pushes a full materialized copy of the tweet into each follower's timeline cache, so reads need no hydration.

steps.decisionPrompt: Where do you store the precomputed timeline, and what do you store in it — full tweets or just ids?

concepts.definition: Writing a new item into every consumer's precomputed view at production time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Trades cheap reads for expensive writes — the right call when reads dominate.
concepts.example: A 200-follower user posts -> 200 cache list-pushes; reads are then O(1).

whyNow: This is the canonical answer to a read-heavy timeline and sets up the celebrity problem you'll solve next.

recap.after: Reads are a single cached list lookup; posting is fast.
recap.newRisk: A celebrity post means tens of millions of cache writes — fanout storms.

deepDives.title: Fanout queue operations
points: Async backlog is the system's hardest operational risk: the 'within seconds' promise is governed by queue lag, not by the queue's existence.
Partition jobs by author (or follower shard) so one hot author can't head-of-line-block the rest, and large-fanout jobs are split into chunked sub-jobs.
Carry a stable tweet_id + fanout_job_id; make each timeline write idempotent by (user_id, tweet_id) so at-least-once retries can't double-insert.
Retry with exponential backoff; route poison jobs to a DLQ after N attempts instead of blocking the partition.
Cap each home_timeline atomically (e.g. LPUSH + LTRIM to ~800) so a burst can't grow a timeline unbounded.
Apply backpressure / rate-limit per author when a burst or a celebrity misclassification spikes job volume.
Alert on fanout lag (enqueue-to-push p99), queue depth, retry rate, duplicate-suppression rate, DLQ growth, and cache write errors.

view.caption: Fanout jobs flow write service -> queue -> workers; the workers resolve followers and push idempotently into capped timeline caches, with retries, a DLQ, and lag/queue-depth alerting around the loop.

traps.trap: Storing full tweet bodies in every follower's timeline.
traps.why: A celebrity tweet would be copied millions of times, wasting memory and making edits/deletes a nightmare.
traps.instead: Store only tweet ids; hydrate bodies from a shared tweet cache on read.

failureDrills.scenario: A fanout worker crashes mid-job after pushing to half the followers.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The job is retried from the queue; pushes are idempotent by (user_id, tweet_id), so the already-pushed half is deduped and only the remaining followers get the id.
failureDrills.mitigation: At-least-once queue + idempotent timeline writes keyed by (user_id, tweet_id); a job that keeps failing lands in a DLQ for inspection rather than blocking its partition.

failureDrills.scenario: Fanout lag spikes during a major event and timelines fall minutes behind.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Lag and queue-depth alerts fire; workers autoscale and large-fanout jobs are chunked so smaller authors aren't starved.
failureDrills.mitigation: Partition by author, chunk celebrity-sized jobs, rate-limit per author, and shed/defer low-priority work under backpressure.

steps.title: 3. The Celebrity Problem: Fanout-on-Read
description: Fanout-on-write breaks for accounts with tens of millions of followers: one post triggers tens of millions of cache writes, saturating workers and delaying everyone.
For these few accounts, do the opposite: don't push at all. Pull their recent tweets at read time and merge them into the requesting user's timeline.
Because there are very few celebrities and they're read constantly, their recent tweets stay hot in cache — the pull is cheap.

view.caption: For celebrity authors the read service skips the precomputed timeline cache and pulls recent tweets from the tweet store, hydrating via the tweet cache.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you decide an account is a 'celebrity', and what threshold avoids both fanout storms and excessive read-time merging?

concepts.definition: Assembling part of a view at read time by pulling from sources instead of precomputing.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids catastrophic write amplification for high-fanout producers.
concepts.example: Skip pushing a 100M-follower account's tweets; pull their last N at read instead.

whyNow: Naming the celebrity problem and solving it asymmetrically is exactly what separates a senior answer from 'just use fanout-on-write'.

recap.before: Every account's tweets were pushed to all followers.
recap.after: Celebrity tweets are pulled at read time; no fanout storm.
recap.newRisk: Reads now have two code paths to reconcile and merge correctly.

traps.why: Then every read fans-in across all followees again — back to the naive bottleneck.
traps.instead: Pull only for the rare high-fanout accounts; push for the vast majority.

steps.title: 4. Hybrid Fanout: Push for the Many, Pull for the Few
description: Combine both: push tweets from normal accounts into follower timelines (cheap reads), and pull recent tweets from the handful of celebrities a user follows at read time.
On read, take the precomputed timeline (push results) and merge in freshly pulled celebrity tweets, then sort newest-first or hand to the ranker.
The threshold is a tuning knob: accounts above ~1M followers (or above some fanout-cost estimate) are flagged is_celebrity and excluded from push.

view.caption: Normal authors fan out through the queue into follower timeline caches, while the read service merges those cached ids with pulled celebrity tweets and hydrates via the tweet cache.

options.name: Hybrid: push for the many, pull for the few (default)
options.description: Normal accounts push tweet ids into follower timelines, celebrities are pulled at read time, and the read path merges and dedups both sources.
pros: Normal accounts push into follower timelines, so the bulk of reads are a single cheap cache lookup.
The handful of celebrities are pulled at read time, so one post never triggers tens of millions of writes.
An is_celebrity threshold is a single knob to balance fanout cost against read-merge cost.
cons: The read path must merge pushed ids with pulled celebrity tweets and dedup correctly.
Maintaining the celebrity flag and re-classifying accounts adds bookkeeping.
Most complex of the three to implement and operate.

view.caption: Non-celebrity tweets are pushed to follower timeline caches and the read service merges them with celebrity tweets pulled from the tweet store at read time.

options.name: Pure fanout-on-write (push for everyone)
options.description: Every post is pushed into all follower timelines, so each read is a single precomputed timeline lookup with no merge.
pros: Every read is the cheapest possible: one precomputed timeline lookup, no merge.
Simple, uniform read path with no celebrity special-casing.
All cost is paid asynchronously at write time.
cons: A celebrity with tens of millions of followers triggers a fanout storm that saturates workers and delays everyone.
Wastes writes on inactive followers who may never read their timeline.
Fanout lag for huge audiences delays when a post appears.

view.caption: Every tweet is fanned out through the queue into all followers' timeline caches, so the read service only reads precomputed tweet ids.

options.name: Pure fanout-on-read (pull for everyone)
options.description: Nothing is precomputed; each read fans in across the user's followees and merges their latest tweets from the tweet store on demand.
pros: Posting is a single write regardless of follower count — no fanout amplification at all.
No precomputed timeline storage to maintain or rebuild.
Always reflects the latest tweets since nothing is precomputed.
cons: Every read fans in across hundreds of followees against the tweet store — too slow at read-heavy scale.
Read cost scales with how many accounts a user follows.
Hard to cache because every user's merge is different.

view.caption: Nothing is precomputed: the read service pulls followees from the social graph and their recent tweets from the tweet store on every timeline read.

steps.decisionPrompt: At read time, how do you merge pushed timeline ids with pulled celebrity tweets without double-counting or missing recent posts?
whyNow: This is the design real systems converge on; presenting it as a deliberate reconciliation of the two patterns is the payoff of the whole case.

recap.after: One timeline correctly merges pushed + pulled tweets.
recap.newRisk: Read path complexity; merge logic and the celebrity threshold need monitoring.

deepDives.title: Choosing the celebrity threshold
points: Threshold trades write cost (fanout) against read cost (pull + merge). Too low and reads get heavy; too high and fanout storms return.
Estimate per-account fanout cost = follower_count × post_rate; flip the strategy when that exceeds the read-merge cost.
The flag can change as accounts grow; handle the transition (backfill or accept a brief gap).

Across the cutoff time, the read merge must avoid duplicates and gaps: record when an author flipped to celebrity and only pull their tweets newer than that cutoff, leaving already-pushed older ids in the timeline. The merge dedups by tweet_id so an id that was both pushed and pulled appears once.

deepDives.title: Bounding the celebrity pull on the read path
points: A user who follows many celebrities could otherwise trigger an unbounded fan-in on every read, which would blow the p99 budget.

Rule of thumb: pull at most the last K tweets (e.g. K=50) from at most M pulled celebrities per read, drawing from the most recently active first; recent celebrity tweets are cache-hot, so these pulls are cheap lookups, not store scans.
Cap and merge: dedupe pulled ids by tweet_id against the pushed timeline ids so an id that was both pushed and pulled appears once, then trim to the page limit before hydration.

If a user follows more than M celebrities, the merge still only competes for the top of the page — older celebrity tweets surface on later pages as the cursor advances, so no candidate is lost, just deferred.

Across a push↔pull transition, pull only tweets newer than the recorded flip cutoff (timestamp or version) and leave already-pushed older ids in place, so the merge has no gap and no duplicate at the boundary.

traps.trap: Recomputing all timelines when a user crosses the celebrity threshold.
traps.why: That is itself a massive fanout event for a popular account.
traps.instead: Stop pushing going forward and rely on the pull path; optionally backfill lazily.

steps.title: 5. Read Path: Hydrate, Rank, Paginate
description: The timeline cache holds tweet ids; hydrate full tweet bodies from a shared tweet cache (cache-aside, falling back to the tweet store on miss).
Filter the hydrated candidates against current state — tombstoned/edited tweets, protected or suspended authors, and unfollowed/muted/blocked authors — then backfill if filtering shortened the page.

Optionally hand candidates to a ranking service for relevance ordering instead of strict reverse-chronological — with a hard timeout so a slow ranker degrades to reverse-chronological rather than breaching the read SLO.

Paginate by snowflake boundary for reverse-chronological feeds; for ranked feeds use an opaque cursor that pins a candidate snapshot plus the last (score, tweet_id) tie-breaker so infinite scroll stays stable as scores change.

The same hydrate-and-filter read path serves the profile timeline (an author-scoped tweet range) and search (an inverted-index query), so both reuse visibility filtering and cursor pagination instead of adding parallel read stacks.

view.caption: The read service reads tweet ids from the timeline cache, hydrates full tweets via the tweet cache (loading from the store on a miss), and scores them with the ranking service.

options.name: Relevance-ranked timeline (default)
options.description: A ranking model scores the small hydrated candidate set so the most engaging tweets surface rather than strictly the newest.
pros: A ranking service scores candidates so the most engaging tweets surface, not just the newest.
Ranking runs on the small hydrated candidate set, staying within the read latency budget.
Typically lifts engagement over strict reverse-chronological.
cons: Needs a ranking model, features, and a serving dependency on the read path.
Less predictable ordering can hide recent tweets users expect to see.
Pagination must be stable even as scores change.

view.caption: The read service hydrates cached timeline ids and sends them to the ranking service to produce a relevance-ranked timeline.

options.name: Reverse-chronological timeline
options.description: Hydrated tweets are sorted purely by their time-ordered snowflake id, newest first, with cursor pagination and no ranking model.
pros: Dead simple: sort hydrated tweets by snowflake id, no ranking model.
Predictable and transparent — users see the latest tweets from who they follow.
Cursor pagination on the time-sortable id is stable under infinite scroll.
cons: Mixes high- and low-value tweets equally; engagement is usually lower than ranked.
A chatty account can dominate the timeline.
Leaves relevance signals unused.

view.caption: The read service returns hydrated tweets straight from the timeline cache in reverse-chronological order, with no ranking step.

options.name: Lightweight heuristic scoring
options.description: Hydrated candidates are scored inline with a cheap formula (recency decay times author affinity times engagement), with no ML serving hop.
pros: Score hydrated candidates with a cheap formula (recency decay × author affinity × engagement) — no ML stack.
Computed inline, so no separate model-serving hop on the read path.
Captures much of the lift of ranking with far less complexity.
cons: Hand-tuned weights drift and need periodic manual retuning.
Can't capture nonlinear signal interactions a learned model would.
Tends to plateau below a trained ranking model.

view.caption: The read service hydrates cached timeline ids and orders them with a lightweight recency-times-affinity heuristic instead of a full ranker.

steps.decisionPrompt: A cached tweet id is only a candidate — what must the read path still check before rendering it, and how do you keep the page full after filtering?
whyNow: Shows you separate the timeline (a list of references) from tweet content (shared, hot), which keeps memory bounded and edits/deletes cheap.

concepts.definition: A roughly time-sortable 64-bit id (timestamp + machine + sequence).
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you sort and paginate timelines by id without a separate timestamp index.
concepts.example: nextCursor = the smallest tweet id returned in this page.

deepDives.title: Visibility filtering: a cached id is a candidate, not a guarantee
points: A tweet id sitting in a timeline cache is only a candidate; the read path must check current state before rendering it.
Filter after hydration: drop tombstoned tweets (status=deleted / deleted_at set), protected or suspended authors, and authors the viewer has unfollowed, muted, or blocked.
Carry author_id in each timeline entry so unfollow/mute/block filtering is a cheap in-memory check, not an extra store hit per tweet.
Filtering can shorten a page; backfill more candidates from the next slice of timeline ids (or the pull path) until the page reaches the requested limit.
Edits show up automatically because bodies are hydrated from the shared store; the writer just invalidates TweetCache and updates Search, never the millions of cached ids.
Deletes set a tombstone and de-index from Search; the id may linger in many timelines but never renders — no synchronous delete-from-every-timeline needed.

view.caption: The read service reads candidate ids from the timeline cache, hydrates bodies from the tweet cache, then filters tombstones and denied authors before returning — backfilling more candidates if the page came up short.

deepDives.title: Cursor stability for ranked vs chronological feeds
points: Reverse-chronological: the cursor is just a snowflake boundary (return ids < lastId), which is naturally stable as new tweets arrive.
Relevance-ranked: a bare snowflake cursor isn't enough because scores reorder candidates between requests.
Use an opaque cursor that pins a candidate-window snapshot id (or version) plus the last (score, tweet_id) tie-breaker, so the next page continues the same ranking.
Pin the candidate set behind that snapshot for a short TTL; if scores are recomputed every request instead, state explicitly that the product accepts some reordering across pages.

deepDives.title: Ranker timeout and fallback (keeping the p99 read SLO)
points: Once relevance ranking is the default, the ranker (and its feature store / model serving) is on the critical read path, so its latency is part of the timeline availability story.
Put a hard timeout on the ranking call (e.g. budget the ranker a fraction of the 200 ms read SLO); if feature fetch or model serving overruns it, abandon the call rather than wait.
On timeout or ranker outage, fall back to reverse-chronological ordering of the already-hydrated candidates — they are in hand, so the page still returns within the SLO, just unranked.
Cheaper intermediate fallbacks: serve a cached per-tweet score if one is fresh enough, or the lightweight heuristic order, before dropping all the way to chronological.
Cap candidate count so the ranker never sees an oversized set, and optionally return a 'degraded ranking' marker so clients/telemetry know the order wasn't personalized.
Alert on ranker timeout rate and fallback rate; a rising fallback rate is a ranking-quality incident even though the timeline read SLO stays intact.

deepDives.title: Profile timeline: an author-scoped read on the same path
points: Viewing one user's profile is simpler than a home timeline: there is no fanout merge — it is just that author's own tweets, newest-first.

Serve it as an author-id + time range query (id < cursor where author_id = userId) over the tweet store, or over a per-author tweet index if the store isn't already partitioned by author; recent ranges are cache-hot.

Reuse the same hydration and visibility filtering as the home path: drop tombstoned/edited tweets, and enforce protected-account rules so a protected author's profile is only visible to approved followers.
Cursor pagination is the plain snowflake boundary (no ranking), so profile timelines need none of the ranked-cursor snapshot machinery.

Cache policy can be lighter than home timelines — a profile is shared across all its viewers (modulo viewer-specific block/mute), so a short-TTL per-author page cache absorbs popular profiles without per-follower precomputation.

view.caption: A profile timeline is an author-scoped range read over the tweet store, hydrated and visibility-filtered through the same read path — no fanout, no per-follower precompute.

deepDives.title: Search: index query plus shared hydration
points: Search has its own write and query path, then reuses the home read path's hydration and visibility filtering — it is not purely the timeline read path.

Write side: the write service asynchronously indexes each new tweet (text, hashtags, author) into an inverted index; this is eventually consistent, so a freshness target of seconds is the honest SLO, not read-your-writes.
Delete side: a tombstoned tweet must be de-indexed (or filtered post-query) so deleted tweets stop appearing in results, mirroring the timeline tombstone behavior.
Query side: the inverted index returns candidate tweet ids ranked by relevance/recency; hydrate those ids through the tweet cache exactly like timeline candidates.

Apply viewer-specific visibility after hydration: protected accounts the viewer can't see, suspended authors, blocks/mutes, and any not-yet-de-indexed tombstones are filtered out before returning — search must not leak tweets a viewer couldn't otherwise see.

Treat search as a secondary requirement: it shares storage and filtering with the core path but is allowed slightly looser freshness, and its relevance ranking is independent of the home-timeline ranker.

view.caption: Tweets are indexed asynchronously at write time; a search query hits the inverted index for candidate ids, then hydrates and visibility-filters them through the shared tweet cache.

recap.before: Timeline is a list of ids.
recap.after: Reads return hydrated, visibility-filtered, optionally ranked, cursor-paginated tweets.
recap.newRisk: Tweet cache misses on cold tweets; ranker latency budget; short pages after filtering.

steps.title: 6. Scaling the Social Graph and Stores
description: Shard the social graph by user id; keep two adjacency indexes (followers-of and following-of) so both fanout (needs followers) and read (needs followees) are single-shard lookups.
Shard tweets by author or tweet id; the time-sortable id makes 'recent tweets by author' a range scan.
Shard the timeline cache by user id; each user's timeline lives on one cache node and is independently evictable/rebuildable.

view.caption: The fanout and read services share the sharded social graph and graph store, the tweet store, and the timeline cache, the three components that must scale as the graph grows.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why maintain two separate adjacency indexes instead of deriving one from the other at query time?
whyNow: Sharding choices follow directly from the two access patterns; getting them aligned avoids cross-shard scatter on the hot paths.

recap.after: Graph, tweets, and timelines all shard on the access key.
recap.newRisk: Hot shards for viral tweets / mega-accounts; rebuilding a lost timeline shard.

bottlenecks.issue: A viral tweet makes one tweet-store shard hot for reads.
bottlenecks.mitigation: It's already hydrated from the shared tweet cache, not the store; the cache absorbs the read fanout.

bottlenecks.issue: Losing a timeline cache shard drops those users' precomputed feeds.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Rebuild lazily on next read via the pull path (followees' recent tweets) and resume push.

bottlenecks.issue: Fanout lag: a burst or celebrity misclassification backs up the queue and delays the 'within seconds' promise.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Partition jobs by author, chunk large-fanout jobs, autoscale workers on lag, and rate-limit per author; alert on enqueue-to-push p99.

bottlenecks.issue: Hot author shard: a high-volume author concentrates writes/reads on one tweet-store shard.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Range-scan recent tweets from the cache, not the store; consider per-author sub-sharding by time for the very largest accounts.

bottlenecks.issue: Graph hot edges: a celebrity's follower list is enormous and read on every one of their posts.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Don't materialize the full follower set for celebrities (they're pulled, not pushed); cache the is_celebrity flag so the read path skips them quickly.

bottlenecks.issue: Cold timeline rebuild is expensive if many shards are lost at once.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Cap rebuild work with a budget (rebuild only the first page on read, fill the rest in the background) so a mass eviction doesn't stampede the pull path.

bottlenecks.issue: Timeline cache memory: ~200B ids across active users strains a single region.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Cap timeline length (~800), evict cold/inactive users to pull-only, and size shards to the active-user working set.

bottlenecks.issue: Regional read availability: a region or cache tier degrades.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Replicate timeline/tweet caches per region for local reads; on cache loss serve a degraded pull-path feed rather than failing the read.

failureDrills.scenario: A timeline cache node is evicted/restarted and a user's timeline is empty.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads fall back to assembling from followees (pull) so the user still sees a feed.
failureDrills.mitigation: Treat the timeline cache as rebuildable derived state, never the source of truth.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Hybrid Fanout Timeline
finalDesign.description: Posts persist to the tweet store and enqueue async fanout; normal accounts push tweet ids into follower timeline caches while celebrities are pulled at read time (bounded to K recent tweets from at most M followed celebrities, deduped by tweet_id). Reads merge pushed + pulled ids, hydrate from the shared tweet cache, filter against current visibility and relationship state (deletes, edits, protected/suspended authors, unfollows/mutes/blocks), optionally rank under a hard timeout that degrades to reverse-chronological, and paginate by snowflake id (ranked feeds use an opaque candidate-snapshot cursor). Media is served via CDN. The profile timeline and search reuse the same hydrate-and-filter path: a profile read is an author-scoped tweet range, and search is an asynchronously-built inverted-index query (with tombstone de-indexing and viewer-specific visibility filtering, eventually consistent within seconds).

view.caption: The client posts through the timeline API to the write service, which persists tweets and media and queues fanout into follower timeline caches; reads merge cached ids with pulled celebrity tweets, hydrate via the tweet cache, and rank, with media served by the CDN and tweets indexed for search.

functional.requirement: Post a tweet visible to followers
functional.how: Write service persists and enqueues fanout; pushed into follower timelines.
Single cached-list read, hydrated and paginated.
Follow adds a graph edge and lazily backfills (or read-merges) the author's recent tweet ids; unfollow removes the edge and is filtered at hydrate time via author_id, with async timeline cleanup.

functional.requirement: Deletes, edits, and blocks are honored
functional.how: Bodies hydrate from the shared store, so edits show automatically; deletes set a tombstone and de-index from search; the read path filters tombstoned/denied authors after hydration.
Author-scoped tweet range read (author_id + id<cursor) over the tweet store, hydrated and visibility-filtered through the same read path; no fanout merge, plain snowflake cursor.

Write service asynchronously indexes each tweet (eventually consistent); search queries the inverted index, then hydrates and viewer-visibility-filters results through the shared read path. Deletes de-index via tombstone.

nonFunctional.how: Precomputed timelines + hot tweet cache make reads O(1) lookups; the ranker runs under a hard timeout and degrades to reverse-chronological so a slow ranker never breaches the SLO.
Async fanout workers process the queue continuously.
Celebrities are pulled at read time, not pushed.
Timeline cache is rebuildable; reads fall back to pull on cache loss.

say: Confirm it's read-heavy (timeline reads >> posts) and that eventual consistency for fanout is acceptable.
Pin the hard targets: read p99 < 200 ms, posts visible within seconds.
Call out the heavy-tailed follower distribution early — it's the crux.
Start naive (assemble on read) to expose the bottleneck.
Introduce fanout-on-write to make reads cheap.
Then name the celebrity problem and introduce fanout-on-read.
Converge on hybrid fanout and the read-time merge.
Discuss the celebrity threshold as a tuning knob.
Cover sharding of graph, tweets, and timelines aligned to access patterns.
Restate how the design hits each requirement.
Acknowledge tradeoffs: read-path complexity, eventual consistency, threshold tuning.
Mention ranking and media/CDN as natural extensions.

expectations: Gets to fanout-on-write and cheap reads.
May not spot the celebrity problem unprompted.
Names and solves the celebrity problem with hybrid fanout.
Shards stores to match access patterns.
Reasons quantitatively about the celebrity threshold.
Treats timelines as rebuildable derived state with a pull fallback.
Discusses ranking, hot-shard mitigation, and consistency tradeoffs explicitly.

followUps: Given the ranker already times out to reverse-chronological, how would you keep its features fresh — online feature store, near-real-time engagement signals — without adding read-path latency?
How would you keep search fresh and protect viewer-specific visibility when the index lags the write path during a spike?
How do you keep timelines bounded in memory (cap length, evict cold users) and size cache shards to the active working set?
How would you support 'see new tweets' real-time updates (push to connected clients)?
How do you make follow backfill cheap when a user follows a very high-volume (but not celebrity) account?
How would you run this multi-region with cache replication and a degraded read mode during a regional outage?
